text: How does it do when torched or heated above 3000f? Would it offgas?

author: @T.D.Barrett73
text: Hey Robert, I purchased a membership to your channel to ask you for help I'm building a graphene heating element. Can you point me in the direction of where to buy graphene sheets for cheap? I need the graphene heating pad, once made, to be 9 inches by 6 inches. Also, what determines the heat capacity of the graphene heating element? Is it simply the amount of electricity pumped into it, or does the graphene quality/thickness etc play a role? The graphene pads on the market are just slightly less hot than what I need

author: @T.D.Barrett73
text: Hey Robert, I jus purchased a membership because I'm very interested in making my own graphene heating pad. The options on the market are not the size I need for my application. I am hoping you can help me with that! I have a few different questions, but I wonder if we could chat instead through a phone call, or email? Not sure YouTube comments are going to be sufficient! Let me know how you want to proceed.

author: @petergiesbers
text: First of all thank you for the inspiration I get from watching your videos. I want to do a test with floor heating using Graphene. I want to use some kind of foil strips so I am looking to buy a roll of Graphene, I have seen graphite foil with Graphene flakes but doubt if that is right. I hope you can point me in the right direction of what the ideal sheet properties should be for my application. Have a great day, best regards Peter from Spain.

author: @mouldykev
text:
Hi again Rob i was going to buy some heat mat's for my son as a Christmas present as he keeps on buying reptiles, now the graphene i will make once my sonnicator arrives and i have ethonol for the carrier but can you please tell me which Alkyd binder you used.
I do plan to get the missus to buy some of your books for me as an xmas prezzie this year so i won't have to ask so many questions....Lol
regards kev.
oooh nearly forgot i just made some graphite flakes from expanded Graphite and the resistance was........0.4 ohms  ....so just under half an ohm then ! at 1cm
The intercalated Graphite was from you know where that i told you about a couple of weeks ago.
I did it by expanding three times, as in expand and roll, expand and roll each time the worms got smaller and the flakes got thinner until now they will not even clump together and they are extremely fine flakes.
I could send you video proof if need be.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't need 'proof' mate - but it would be interesting to see your experiment - upload so we can all share

replies.author: @mouldykev

author: @randytravis5893
text: Hi, Its good to see you still hard at work. I have something you should find very interesting. There is a man here in America that is called Manoj Bhargava. He is the owner of a product called 5 hour energy. It is a very popular product here in America and it makes huge amounts of money. Mr Bhargava gives most of his profits away to researchers researching ideas to help 3rd world people, who need energy and water. One of his big projects is to make graphine cables to transfer heat directly from deep in the earth to the surface for heat / energy transfer.  I bet he would be very interested to speak with you. He likes to hire innovators, "or fund them," who have new ideas, and likes to give lots of money and freedom to his researchers in their research.Please watch his video, it is very interesting, and I think you will like this guy, and I bet he would like you too. The video is called  Billions in change.Its not that long, and it was so interesting, it was over before I knew it.One of his big projects now is to use heat energy, deep in the earth, by direct heat transfer. He want to do this by using graphine cables.He is trying to developing graphine cables by making threads of graphine, then to make a cable. If this isn't nessary to transfer heat, then I bet you could make it cheeper by just using you graphine ink or some other method you know of.Please give it a look see, and thanks for all your great videos.All the best.Randy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Randy Travis if he drops me a line i would be happy to chat to him but i never chase folks mate

author: @lonerayven

author: @HUGHESY012
text:
I have now watched all your videos and find them some of the most interesting and fun items items on You Tube your enthusiasm is infectious. I am an engineer myself with a number of patents this subject seems brimming with opportunity and thanks for bringing them to me in a simple easy to follow format.
Since viewing this video I am now looking at a heating system for my house from a UK supplier my question is the technology there yet or should I wait? I will keep following your videos and if you have any more info on heating elements I would appreciate it. One last question how expensive is the graphene ink I would love to make a portable heater just to try it? 

Tom

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Jess Hughes Hiya Tom - the basic technology is pretty mature ( about 10-15 years old i think) this is just a wrinkle on that - any conductive/ resistive ink will do this job and i do a video on how to make your own

Andy

replies.author: @JackMosel
replies.text:
Leonard Janus I was wondering the same exact thing. Binding (connecting) the copper bus to the Graphene. 

Can you give me your contact info please? I’m going to attempt residential / Solar / Wall / Thermostat / DC Battery / Transformer  to Graphene surfaces here in NY. I tried to reach to Carbon 4 in Germany but they say they refuse to export/import into USA due to UL/CE listing issues. Of course.. USA is Sooo progressive... ridiculous. I’ll have a Master Electrician friend sign off on field installation(s). I found a Nuclear Grade Graphene Foil U.S. manufacturer. They gladly sent me a sample. I believe it will provide what I’m looking for in therms of X,Y,Z efficiency as well as <10 Ohm resistance. Anyone’s response for off the shelf sourcing would be greatly appreciated. 

Great Channel! 🥳🇺🇸

author: @CoyoteGenius314
text: Check your resistance at high temp? Carbon unlike Nichrome or iron wire resistance drops with temp rise, should be about 1 ohm per 100 C. What this means is you could heat to temp. in parallel then switch to series to drive a load. Depending how you look at it, heat or load energy is free! Dare I say free energy proof?
